coredata.dc:title: A smartphone-assisted ultrasensitive detection of acrylamide in thermally processed snacks using CQD@Au NP integrated FRET sensor
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Pattnayak, Bibek Chaw
Mohapatra, Sasmita
coredata.dc:description: Selective, sensitive, and accurate detection of acrylamide (AA) in thermally processed food is a great challenge for food safety. This paper describes a “turn-on” fluorescence strategy to detect AA in real samples. Herein, the fluorescence intensity of glutathione-modified carbon quantum dots (GSHCQDs) was quenched initially upon the addition of gold nanoparticles (Au NPs) via fluorescence resonance electron transfer (FRET) to form a quenched GSHCQD-Au nanoprobe. When AA was introduced to the quenched GSHCQD-Au nanoprobe, the strong thiol-ene Michael addition (M−A) reaction among the -SH group of GSHCQD and AA occurred which releases GSHCQD to the medium and FL intensity at 520 nm is regained. The GSHCQD-Au nanoprobe can detect the AA in a normal aqueous solution (pH 7) selectively over a short response time of 5 min. Under the optimized conditions, the detection limit of AA was obtained to be 0.12 pM, over a wide linear range of 0–200 nM. Especially, this FRET-based sensing method was utilized successfully for the sensitive detection of AA using an RGB app installed on a smartphone, opening a new approach for the smart sensing of food contaminants.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dot
Acrylamide
Gold nanoparticles
Potato chips
RGB analysis

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of water extract from potato chips and HPLC conditions
Optimisation of pH, response time and detection of AA

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Department of Science and Technology, Ministry of Science and Technology, India

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work is financially supported by SERB, DST, Govt. of India (CRG/2018/001717). BC thanks DST-INSPIRE for providing fellowship (IF190390).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic presentation of synthesis of GSHCQD-Au nanoprobe and its detection towards acrylamide.
(a) UV spectra of GSHCQD. (b) Florescence emission spectra of GSHCQD at different excitation wavelength. (c) TEM image of GSHCQD. (d) HR-TEM image of GSHCQD.
(a) TEM image of Au NPs (b-d) TEM image of GSHCQD-Au nanoprobe at different magnification.
(a) FL spectra of GSHCQD and UV–vis spectra of Au NPs. (b) FL decay plot of GSHCQD and GSHCQD-Au nanoprobe. (c) FL spectra of GSHCQD in the presence of Au NPs. (d) Plot of F/F0 vs Au NPs conc.
(a) FL recovery of GSHCQD-Au NP with the increase in AA conc. (b) Linear plot between F/F0 and AA conc.
(a) RGB analysis for the long UV irradiated colour change of GSHCQD-Au probe solution upon addition of AA. (b) Plot of linear relationship between the G/B value and the AA concentration.

head.ce:title: A smartphone-assisted ultrasensitive detection of acrylamide in thermally processed snacks using CQD@Au NP integrated FRET sensor

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, National Institute of Technology Rourkela, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, National Institute of Technology Rourkela, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Selective, sensitive, and accurate detection of acrylamide (AA) in thermally processed food is a great challenge for food safety. This paper describes a “turn-on” fluorescence strategy to detect AA in real samples. Herein, the fluorescence intensity of glutathione-modified carbon quantum dots (GSHCQDs) was quenched initially upon the addition of gold nanoparticles (Au NPs) via fluorescence resonance electron transfer (FRET) to form a quenched GSHCQD-Au nanoprobe. When AA was introduced to the quenched GSHCQD-Au nanoprobe, the strong thiol-ene Michael addition (M−A) reaction among the -SH group of GSHCQD and AA occurred which releases GSHCQD to the medium and FL intensity at 520 nm is regained. The GSHCQD-Au nanoprobe can detect the AA in a normal aqueous solution (pH 7) selectively over a short response time of 5 min. Under the optimized conditions, the detection limit of AA was obtained to be 0.12 pM, over a wide linear range of 0–200 nM. Especially, this FRET-based sensing method was utilized successfully for the sensitive detection of AA using an RGB app installed on a smartphone, opening a new approach for the smart sensing of food contaminants.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Acrylamide (IUPAC name, 2-propanamide, AA), a potential cancer-causing agent is produced from the Maillard reaction between the amino acid “asparagine” and the reducing sugar during food processing above 120 ˚C [1,2]. The Swedish National Food Administration reported a high level of AA in baked and fried foods, which is much greater than the acceptable limit i.e., 1 µg/L as recommended by WHO [3]. Oral consumption of AA clearly shows its toxic effect on the nervous system, reproductive system, and developing capacity [4]. Besides, acrylamide has a very smaller molecular weight of 71.08 g mol−1, a low vapor pressure of 230 Pa, fast reactivity, and sensitivity, which creates very troublesome for qualitative as well as quantitative analysis. So, it is very much essential to develop a precise, anti-interfering, and sensitive test for the fast and precise detection of acrylamide. Conventional techniques such as high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [5], gas chromatography (GC) [6], and LC-ESI-MS-Triple Quadrupole spectroscopy [7], have been used as the primary methods to detect acrylamide content in food. Although these instrument-based techniques have very good precision and sensitivity, drawbacks like sophisticated and expensive instruments, lengthy experimental protocols, and the need for highly trained personnel limit their practical application. Analytical sensing tools that can convert concentration-based chemical information into detectable signals are of emerging interest due to their sensitivity and easy operation [8]. In the sensing process, molecular recognition is the crucial aspect which in turn generates a physicochemical indication, which is further transformed into a measurable signal by a transducer [9–11]. Fluorescence spectroscopy appeared as a unique analytical tool having better accuracy, sensitivity, and selectivity. The sensitivity of fluorescence sensors can be increased by improving nanomaterials’ optical properties and applying a well-defined molecular recognition protocol. In this way, the fluorophore can be modified with specific functional groups to interact with acrylamide hence enhancing the sensitivity [12,13]. In reported optical sensors for acrylamide, the sensing mechanism is based on the polymerization-induced distance enhancement among amino-functionalized CdSe/ZnS QDs in presence of AA [14], competitive bonding of FAM-csDNA and AA to ss-DNA resulting in variable fluorescence emission [15], phosphorescence quenching of l-cysteine-covered Mn-doped Zinc sulfide QDs [16], colour change of Au NP owing to the increase in distance between GSH modified Au NP in presence of acrylamide via thiol-ene Michael addition [17,18]. However, in all single fluorescence probe sensing methods the fluorescence intensity is highly perturbed by environmental factors which results in lack of reproducibility in the sensing method.

On the otherhand, Fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) is a unique photophysical process that refers to the transfer of energy from the donor to the energy acceptor at a close distance (i.e. 1–10 nm). Among several organic fluorophores, inorganic quantum dots, Au NP, and rare earth materials, carbon quantum dots show encouraging performance as a FRET donor or acceptor because of their unique broad absorption as well as excitation-dependent emission property [19,20]. When the fluorescence emission band of donor overlaps with the absorption band of acceptor molecule, FRET based quenching takes place effectively [21]. More specifically emission spectrum of carbon quantum dots coincides with the absorption spectrum of gold nanoparticles in the blue region. Since both CQDs and Au NPs can be surface-functionalized easily, the CQD-Au NP FRET sensor can be used to detect various types of hazardous ions and biological molecules [22–24]. To investigate the utility of this concept in AA detection, we constructed a FRET-based sensing platform using –SH functionalized CD as donor and citrate stabilized Au NP as the acceptor components.

In the present study, we hydrothermally synthesized -SH functionalized carbon quantum dot from the natural precursor chlorophyll and glutathione. The citrate stabilized Au NPs have been taken as a fluorescence quencher by forming a FRET pair through a strong Au-S bond with –SH functionalized CQD. The molecular recognition between the FRET sensor and AA has been achieved by the thiol-ene Michael addition reaction. Thus in presence of AA, the thiol-functionalized CQD was desorbed from the surface of gold NPs and the quenched fluorescence signal of the CQD-Au nanoprobe was enhanced. Besides, the colour of the probe solution under long wavelength UV could be converted to RGB values, which were used for the quantitative analysis of the AA through an RGB colour detector app using a smartphone.

Acrylamide and glutathione were supplied by Sigma Aldrich. Sodium chloride, silver nitrate, potassium chloride, iron (III) chloride, copper (II) chloride, calcium chloride, sucrose, and acetic acid were supplied by Merck ltd. Methylacrylamide, acrylic acid, l-histidine, l-alanine, and d-glucose were purchased from Himedia. Baked bread and potato chips were purchased from the local market of NIT Rourkela. All the chemicals were utilized without further refinement and mili-pore water (18.2 MΩ) was used during the experiment.

Chlorophyll was extracted from papaya leaves (Carica papaya). First fresh green papaya leaves were brought, cleaned thoroughly with water and crushed into pieces. The crushed leaves were kept in ethanol overnight. Then the ethanol part was filtered and evaporated to get the chlorophyll. Again the obtained chlorophyll solution was treated with EDTA solution to get magnesium-free chlorophyll. The obtained chlorophyll solution was freeze-dried and stored in the freezer for further use.

0.1 g of chlorophyll was taken in a beaker and dissolved with 20 mL ethanol and 40 mL of water. To the solution, 1 mL of ethylenediamine and 0.01 g of glutathione were added with constant stirring. The prepared solution was transferred to an autoclave (Berghof, Germany) and treated hydrothermally at 180 ℃ for 12 h. Then the solution was cooled down to room temperature naturally and filtered using a 0.22 µm filter membrane (M.W. cut-off 12,000–14,000 Da) to remove the bigger particles. After filtration, the solution was put in dialysis for 24 h to get particles of small size. Finally, a brown color solution was obtained and lyophilized for further use.

Au NPs were synthesized by the reduction of HAuCl4 using sodium citrate as a reducing agent. 240 mg of HAuCl4 was mixed in 500 mL of distilled water and was stirred till mixing. Then the solution was heated to boiling with the addition of 50 mL of sodium citrate solution (1%) and the stirring was continued for the next 1 h [25]. After 1 h, a wine-red solution was obtained with an absorption maximum at 520 nm. The molar extinction coefficient (ε) of the Au NP at 520 nm is about 2.7 × 108 M−1 cm−1. According to Beer Lambert's law, the final concentration of synthesized Au NP was calculated to be 4.4 nM [26].

The morphological study of freeze-dried GSHCQD and GSHCQD-Au nanoprobe was done by transmission electron microscopy (FEI, Tecnai G2 TF30-ST). Surface functionalization was recorded using an FTIR spectrophotometer (IRAffinity-1S, Shimadzu). In all the sensing experiments, Horiba FluoroMax-4 fluorimeter spectrophotometer was used to collect the emission spectrum. The X-ray diffraction pattern was collected using an X-ray diffractometer (Bruker AXS D8 Advance). The absorbance of the liquid samples was recorded with the help of a UV–vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu UV-2600). The surface charges were determined using a Zetasizer instrument (Malvern). The X-ray photoelectron spectra were recorded using X-ray photoelectron spectrophotometry (PHI 5000 Versa Probe-III). The photos were taken under a UV lamp (365 nm) by using a smartphone. RGB analysis was done using the RGB colour detector app installed on an android smartphone.

In the experiment, 20 µL of GSHCQD (0.01 mg/mL) was added to 3 mL of water, along with that 280 µL of Au NPs solution was added and shaken for 5 min at 37 ℃. Then 200 µL of acrylamide solution of different concentrations was mixed and shaken for the next 5 min. The PL spectra were recorded at λem = 520 nm and λex = 460 nm. To investigate the selectivity method, some standard potential chemicals like sucrose, l-histidine, l-alanine, d-glucose, and acetic acid were also experimented at the same experimental conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of water extract from potato chips and HPLC conditions
ce:section.ce:para: First, potato chips and fried breadsticks of 1 g each were crushed into a centrifuge tube and 10 mL of methanol–water solution (1:1, v/v) was added to it. The tube vibrated strongly for 2 min. Then the solution was centrifuged at 9,000 rpm for 15 min. A thin layer of white fat was discarded and the supernatant solution was transferred to a beaker and heated at 75 ˚C for evaporation. The above steps were repeated two times for the extraction. The cleaning processes were referred to as per the FDA regulation [27]. The collected residue was mixed in 1 mL of water and worked up with hexane two times for defatting. The collected solution was kept at 4 ℃ for further experiments. The samples were spiked with different concentrations of AA and the experiment was repeated at least 5 times. The fluorescent detection condition was the same as described above. The fluorometric detection was done with excitation at 460 nm.

For the determination of AA, a high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) (Agilent 1200) equipped with a UV spectrophotometer operating at 210 nm was used for the quantitative determination of AA. 50% deionized water and 50% acetonitrile were used as a mobile phase at a flow rate of 1 mL min−1. Standard AA solution was used to determine the AA concentrations in real samples.

The leaf extract chlorophyll shows peaks at 1163, 1445, 1789, 2852, 2922, and 3380 cm−1, which are attributed to the presence of CO, CH, CO, –CH2, –CH3, and –OH groups respectively. Compared to the precursor used, the FTIR spectrum of GSHCQD revealed that the broad transmittance at 3200–3600 cm−1 is due to the presence of –NH and –OH functionalization. The peak at 1625 cm−1 is ascribed to the presence of the –COOH group, whereas, the new peak at 2550 cm−1 is attributed to the -SH functionalization of the GSHCQD. The presence of –NH, –OH, and –COOH groups increases the water solubility and stability of the CQDs. The TEM image of GSHCQD displays a uniform distribution of GSHCQD having a diameter of range 4–6 nm (Fig. 2c). The high-resolution TEM image shows a lattice fringe of 0.26 nm (Fig. 2d). Further, the XRD analysis of GSHCQD shows a peak at 23.60, which is ascribed to the [002] graphitic planes of CQD (Fig. S2) [28].

Agreeing with the TEM results, the DLS results show that GSHCQD possesses a hydrodynamic size of about 34 nm for one day (Fig. S3), because the hydrodynamic diameter possesses a larger size than that of geometric diameter due to the hydration effect. Still GSHCQD shows a narrow size distribution with an average diameter of about 39 nm after 30 d. The zeta potential of GSHCQDs at pH 7 after 1 d and 30 d are found to be +2.5 mV and +0.29 mV respectively (Fig. S4). All the results above show that GSHCQDs are surrounded by positively charged groups which enhance the dispersibility and stability of GSHCQDs.

The TEM image illustrates a uniform dispersion of Au NPs within a size range of 4–9 nm (Fig. 3 a). FTIR spectra show that GSHCQD exhibits a sharp peak at 2550 cm−1, which is due to the presence of -SH functionalization (Fig. S5). The disappearance of the peak with the addition of Au-NPs indicates the formation of a GSHCQD-Au nanoprobe. The TEM images (Fig. 3b-d, S6) illustrate the formation of the GSHCQD-Au nanoprobe over a dimension range of 10–20 nm. The HRTEM images show a lattice fringe of 0.26 nm and 0.23 nm, which corresponds to the [002] plane of graphitic carbon and [111] plane of gold nanoparticles respectively [28,29]. Further XPS study was conducted to analyze the valence bond and composition of the GSHCQD-Au nanoprobe (Fig.S7). The XPS spectrum of the GSHCQD-Au nanoprobe showed characteristic peaks at 84.4, 162.5, 284.1, 399.3, and 531.2 eV, which attributed to Au 4f, S 2p, C 1s, N 1s, and O 2p respectively. The high-resolution scan of the C 1s spectrum contains five peaks at 284.5, 285.3, 285.8, 286.6, and 288.5, which are attributed to the bond energy of CC/CC, CS, CN, C-OH, and CO respectively. The high-resolution O 1s spectrum having 531.2, 532.1, 533.1, and 533.6 are ascribed to COC/C-OH, CO, CO, and O-CO respectively. The high-resolution N 1s XPS spectrum shows peaks at 400.2, 399.7, 398.9, and 396.7 which are assigned to the NH, graphitic N, pyrrolic N, and pyridinic N respectively [30]. The S 2p high-resolution spectrum shows two bands at 162.7 and 163.5 eV are assigned to S-Au and SC bonds respectively. In addition, the Au 4f binding region can be divided into two peaks at 83.6 and 84.7 eV, which are ascribed to Au (0) states and S-Au (I) respectively [31,32]. All these information confirms the establishment of a GSHCQD-Au conjugated probe where Au is connected to GSHCQD through the SH group. The absolute quantum yield of GSHCQD was calculated to be 30.12% and 19.2%under the excitation of 340 nm and 460 nm respectively.

Generally, the FRET process depends upon two factors: the mutual overlap of spectra between the emission spectrum of the donor and the absorption of the acceptor and there must be a mutual distance (<10 nm) between the donor and acceptor.

From Fig. 4 a, it can be seen that the UV–vis absorption spectrum of the citrate stabilized Au NPs overlaps with the fluorescence emission spectrum of GSHCQD (λex = 460 nm). At pH 7, the zeta potential of Au NPs and CQDs are found to be −42.7 mV and +2.5 mV, indicating the negatively charged surface of Au NPs and the positively charged sphere of GSHCQD. The positively charged sphere of GSHCQD can adsorb on the rough surface of citrate stabilized Au-NPs by replacing the citrate ligands from its surface by using Au-S chemistry [33], and FL intensity is dropped because of energy transfer from GSHCQD to Au NP. To further investigate the fluorescence quenching mechanism, the fluorescence lifetime of GSHCQD and GSHCQD-Au NPs were studied. As illustrated in Fig. 4b, the fluorescence lifetime of GSHCQD and GSHCQD-Au NPs were found to be 6.2 and 4.9 ns respectively. The fluorescence lifetime of GSHCQD changes with the addition of Au NPs, which suggests the dynamic quenching behaviour of CQD [34]. Using the lifetime data of GSHCQD donor (td) and GSHCQD-Au nanoprobe (donor–acceptor, td-a), the FRET efficiency (E) was calculated to be 20.96% using equation (1).
(1)
E
=
1 -
t
d - a
/
t d

With the addition of Au NPs to GSHCQD, the emission intensity of CQD at 520 nm gradually decreases. As shown in Fig. 4c-d, the quenching efficiency was determined by plotting a graph of F/F0 against Au NPs concentration, where F = fluorescence intensity of the GSHCQD upon the addition of Au NPs and F0 = fluorescence intensity of the GSHCQD in the absence of Au. It is found that, when the concentration of Au NPs increases to 1.4 nM, the PL intensity of GSHCQD was quenched to nearly 55%. Besides, the ionic interaction between the oppositely charged GSHCQD (+2.5 mV) and Au NPs (−42.7 mV) contributes to the quenching as well as a sensing mechanism. The better quenching percentages display a good transfer of energy between the GSHCQD and Au NPs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimisation of pH, response time and detection of AA
ce:section.ce:para: When acrylamide was added to the FRET pair, there was a gradual increase in the fluorescence intensity at 520 nm. The intensity of GSHCQD-Au NPs decreased adequately and an equilibrium is reached in no time and to avoid experimental failures 5 min was preferred as a standard time to reduce the operational errors. After the addition of AA to the FRET pair, the quenched fluorescence intensity increased in 1 min, but the greater intensity is observed after 5 min with a constant state (Fig. S11). From the above standardization experiments, it was found that 5 min was the incubation time for both the first part and second part of the reaction to take place without error.

The effect of pH on the fluorescence intensity was further investigated by changing the pH of the solution. As can be illustrated in fig. S9, the PL intensity of CQD was highly dependent on the pH of the solution. The fluorescence intensity increased with the rise in the pH value, touched a peak for pH 6 and pH 7, and then decreased. During the FRET, greater quenching is seen at pH 7. A greater difference in fluorescence intensity between the GSHCQD and GSHCQD-Au was observed at pH 7. Since GSHCQD was positively charged at pH 7 and Au NPs were negatively charged at the same pH, the binding of the GSHCQD-Au nanoprobe was facilitated. At acidic pH, the -ve charge on the Au NPs might decrease and affect the stability of Au NPs, which in turn affects the FRET between GSHCQD and Au NPs [33]. Therefore, for the experimental analysis, a solution of pH 7 was preferable.

Under an optimum reaction condition, the enhancement in emission intensity of the GSHCQD-Au NP FRET pair in presence of various concentrations of AA is shown in Fig. 5 a. The emission intensity change of GSHCQD-Au NP versus AA concentration clearly illustrates a linear increase in the range of 0–200 nM (R2 = 0.99) (Fig. 5b). The LOD value was found to be 0.1 pM, which is calculated by following our previous publication [35]. In contrast, when AA was added to only GSHCQD the enhancement of this LOD value is comparable to the previously reported methods to detect AA in the aqueous phase (Table S1). Compared with other methods, the methods demonstrated here did not need any expensive equipment, harsh conditions, or complicated process.

When the acrylamide was mixed with the above solution, the emission intensity of the GSHCQD-Au NP FRET sensor was significantly enhanced due to the discontinuation of energy transfer from GSHCQD to AuNP. From FTIR, the presence of –SH groups on CQD was evident. When AA is added to the sensing medium the thiol-ene M−A reaction occurred among the -SH (thiol group) of GSHCQD and the alkene group of AA. As a result, the FRET pair between the GSHCQD-Au NPs is broken and there is some enhancement in the quenched peak of the GSHCQD-Au NPs in a concentration-dependent manner. As per the report by Zhou etal, butylamine is used as a catalyst in thiol-ene M−A reaction [36]. In our work no extra amine has been used to accelerate the reaction between thiol terminated carbon dots GSHCQD and acrylamide. However, during the synthesis of GSHCQD ethylenediamine was used as a precursor. So there is possibility of surface adsorbed ethylenediamine on GSHCQD which is indicated by zetapotential + 2.5 at pH 7. The presence of ethylenediamine creates alkaline environment and catalyses thiol-ene M−A reaction for which there was rapid increase in fluorescence intensity within 1 min and it goes on up to 5 min with a constant rate. Hence, the present sensing method is suitable to assay the concentration of AA. To prove our proposition, we did one control experiment. When the sensor probe was constituted taking only chlorophyll-derived CQD and Au NP, no considerable change in the fluorescence intensity of the sensing medium was observed, which indicates almost no interaction of the CQD-Au NP probe with AA (Fig. S12). The proposed sensing mechanism is also evidenced by the hydrodynamic size analysis (Fig. S13). When the concentration of AA varied from 100 nm to 500 nm, the hydrodynamic size of the GSHCQD-Au nanoprobe was reduced from 400 nm to 170 nm, with some extra peaks at the low size region. At higher AA concentrations, the additional peaks are attributed to the presence of disintegrated GSHCQD and Au nanoparticles.

Based on the above observation, a schematic diagram was developed for the detection of AA is shown in Fig. 1. When Au NPs were added to the -SH functionalized GSHCQD, the CQD was adsorbed on the surface of the Au NPs through the Au-S chemistry, resulting in the transfer of energy between the GSHCQD and the Au NPs. The PL intensity of GSHCQD was quenched by Au NPs due to FRET. This quenched state of the GSHCQD is called “off state”. When the AA was added to the “off state”, the FRET pair was destroyed and underwent a reaction among the -SH group of GSHCQD and the alkenyl group of AA through thiol-ene reaction, resulting in the “turn-on” of the fluorescence emission of the GSHCQD.

As a concluding check of the FRET mechanism, we calculated the donor–acceptor distance (r) from the FRET efficiency (E) and the Förster distance (R0) measurements by using the following equation-.
(2)
E
=
1
/
1
+
r
/
R 0
6
in
Å
u n i t
Where the Förster distance (R0) was measured using Felix GX 4.1.2 software.
These donor–acceptor distances were calculated to be in the range of 10–100 Å (Table S2), which is consistent with the requirements for the FRET mechanism [20].

To evaluate the specificity of the FRET-based fluorescence detection system, the fluorescence response of the GSHCQD-Au NP probe towards different structurally analogous molecules and suspected cations was studied under the same conditions. For the selectivity study, sucrose, l-histidine, l-alanine, d-glucose, acetic acid, acrylic acid, methylacrylamide, Na+, K+, Ca2+, Fe3+, Cu2+ and Ag2+ of concentration 10 times higher than that of the analyte AA were studied. As shown in Fig. S15a, under a similar set of experimental conditions, no significant change in FL intensity of the GSHCQD-Au nanoprobe was seen after adding the different analogs. Sucrose, l-Histidine, l-Alanine, acetic acid, and d-Glucose are common chemicals found in foods without having α,β-unsaturated alkene group, resulting in no MA reaction with the -SH group of GSHCQDs. Therefore, GSHCQD can adsorb on the Au NPs' surface after the reaction, leading to the FRET quenching. For acrylic acid having electron deficient α,β-unsaturated alkene group also leads to a better FRET quenching. The reason may be attributed to their acidity, which changed the pH of the MA reaction condition and caused no reaction [33]. Methylacrylamide, which differs from acrylamide by a methyl group also showed about 90% lower response than that of acrylamide. The cause may be attributed to the presence of an electron-donating methyl group of methylacrylamide, which enhances the difficulty of the -SH group of GSHCQD to attack. As a result of which, there was no significant change in PL intensity of the other molecules as compared to AA. The interference test was conducted by mixing an equal amount of AA with the other potential interfering molecules and it was found that in the presence of other similar structural molecules, the response of AA was negligibly affected as compared (Fig. S15b). The above experimental results indicated that the FRET pair-based designed detection system possessed great selectivity and better sensing of the GSHCQD-Au nanoprobe towards AA.

The realistic application of GHCQD-Au nanoprobe towards AA sensing was verified in real food samples. For this purpose, the water extract of potato chips and fried breadsticks was spiked with the known concentration of AA, whereas non-spiked solutions were taken as standard references. Various concentrations of AA were added to the standard samples and were analyzed. Table S3 presented the percentage of recovery over a broad range of concentrations. The recovery percentage varied from 98.6% to 102.6%, which indicates that the probe has better sensitivity, and accuracy and can be utilized to detect AA in real samples.

As compared to the HPLC analytical technique, no AA was found in the fried bread sticks and potato chips. As shown in Table S3, the HPLC analysis method showed a recovery percentage of 99.05% ̴ 100.9% for the real sample. The results showed the uniformity of FRET with HPLC. Additionally, our proposed analytical method needed a short time consuming (<10 min) than that of the HPLC method (<60 min). The FRET-based sensing method was proved to be accurate, rapid, and stable as compared to other techniques.

To justify the application of our method in adopting a general device-based detection system, we performed an automated colour signal readout by performing RGB analysis of the final probe + AA solution photos under the irradiation of long UV (365 nm) in a closed wooden box. An RGB colour detector app was installed on a smartphone for the RGB analysis. As shown in Fig. 6 a, with the increase in the concentration of AA, the colour of the probe solution under long UV changes from deep blue to bright blue, indicating that the change in G/B values is associated with the change in AA concentrations. Results show that when the concentration of AA ranged from 10 to 80 nM, a good linear relationship was obtained with a correlation efficiency of 0.9859 (Fig. 6b). Therefore, it is concluded that this smartphone-based RGB analysis is very simple to operate over a wide linear range. As a consequence, the AA concentration in the blank and spiked samples was analyzed using the smartphone-based RGB analysis and the results were compared with the PL spectra to check the accuracy and precision. Table S3 indicated that the RGB analysis results of various spiked samples are in good agreement with the results obtained using PL spectra. With a recovery percentage of 96.8–106.4, the smartphone-based RGB analysis proved its practical use in real samples.

A stable, sensitive, and selective system was developed for the rapid detection of AA ground on the FRET mechanism. The fluorescence intensity of the GSHCQD is gradually decreased in the presence of Au NPs through the FRET phenomenon. The inhibition of the FRET leads to an increase in fluorescence signal for the quantitative detection of AA. The excellent property of GSHCQD, which can detect AA with high selectivity was investigated in the presence of common food chemicals and ions as possible interferents. Based on Au-S chemical bonding and thiol-ene M−A reaction between GSHCQD and AA, the designed method showed a linear relationship with the AA concentration over a linear range of 0–200 nM and a LOD of 0.1 pM. Fried bread sticks and potato chips selected as real samples were contaminated with AA and the AA extracted from the samples was quantified by both the fluorometric method and the HPLC method. Besides, a simple and portable smartphone-assisted RGB analysis was established for the on-site detection of AA. Hence, establishing a switch off–on probe for an easy, eco-friendly, and cost-effective detection of AA. Moreover, considering the use of a smartphone-assisted detector this proposed FRET-based method will have great application for the detection of AA in real food samples.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is financially supported by SERB, DST, Govt. of India (CRG/2018/001717). BC thanks DST-INSPIRE for providing fellowship (IF190390).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2022.122009.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Unraveling the Serum Metabolomic Profile of Acrylamide-Induced Cardiovascular Toxicity
J. Agric. Food. Chem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Wang, X. Chen, S. Wu, W. Jia, J. Jiao, Y. Zhang, Unraveling the Serum Metabolomic Profile of Acrylamide-Induced Cardiovascular Toxicity, Journal of Agricultural and Food Chemistry, 69 (2021) 12012-12020.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Acrylamide is formed in the Maillard reaction
Nature

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: D.S. Mottram, B.L. Wedzicha, A.T. Dodson, Acrylamide is formed in the Maillard reaction, Nature, 419 (2002) 448-449.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Levels of acrylamide in foods included in ‘the first French total diet study on infants and toddlers’
Food Chem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Lambert, C. Inthavong, F. Hommet, J.-C. Leblanc, M. Hulin, T. Guérin, Levels of acrylamide in foods included in ‘the first French total diet study on infants and toddlers’, Food Chemistry, 240 (2018) 997-1004.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The carcinogenicity of acrylamide, Mutation Research/Genetic Toxicology and Environmental
Mutagenesis

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J.M. Rice, The carcinogenicity of acrylamide, Mutation Research/Genetic Toxicology and Environmental Mutagenesis, 580 (2005) 3-20.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Determination of acrylamide concentration in processed food products using normal phase high-performance liquid chromatography
Afr. J. Biotechnol.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Prashant, S. Pragya, R. R Balaji, Determination of acrylamide concentration in processed food products using normal phase high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC), African Journal of Biotechnology, 9 (2010) 8085-8091.

Food Control

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S.-y. Sun, Y. Fang, Y.-m. Xia, A facile detection of acrylamide in starchy food by using a solid extraction-GC strategy, Food Control, 26 (2012) 220-222.

Food Chem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: E.L. De Paola, G. Montevecchi, F. Masino, D. Garbini, M. Barbanera, A. Antonelli, Determination of acrylamide in dried fruits and edible seeds using QuEChERS extraction and LC separation with MS detection, Food Chemistry, 217 (2017) 191-195.

New Developments in Nanosensors for Pharmaceutical Analysis

Int. J. Environ. Anal. Chem.

IEEE Access

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: P.K.D. Pramanik, A. Solanki, A. Debnath, A. Nayyar, S. El-Sappagh, K.-S. Kwak, Advancing modern healthcare with nanotechnology, nanobiosensors, and internet of nano things: Taxonomies, applications, architecture, and challenges, IEEE Access, 8 (2020) 65230-65266.

Advances in Food and Nutrition Research

RSC Adv.

J. Chromatogr. A

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Feinberg, Validation of analytical methods based on accuracy profiles, Journal of Chromatography A, 1158 (2007) 174-183.

Biosens. Bioelectron.
